title: C# Advanced — Paper 4
description: Advanced C# covering LINQ Advanced Operators, Lambda & Functional Patterns, Delegates, Events, Async/Await & Task Basics, Reflection, Attributes, Extension Methods, Expression Trees, and Dynamic Types.

questions.text: What does async/await do to a method in C#?

options.text: It creates a new thread for each awaited operation.
It transforms the method into a state machine that suspends execution at each 'await' point, freeing the current thread while waiting, then resuming when the awaited task completes.
It makes the method execute synchronously on a background thread.
It blocks the calling thread until the operation completes.

questions.text: What is the purpose of ConfigureAwait(false) in async methods?

options.text: It disables the async/await pattern for a specific call.
It tells the awaiter not to capture the current synchronization context, allowing the continuation to run on any thread pool thread, which avoids deadlocks in library code and improves performance.
It ensures the continuation always runs on the UI thread.
It configures the task to not throw exceptions.

questions.text: What is the difference between LINQ's SelectMany with a collection selector vs a result selector overload?

options.text: They produce identical output regardless of overload.
The collection selector overload flattens sub-sequences into one; the result selector overload additionally projects each (outer, inner) pair through a result function before returning.
The result selector overload only works with IQueryable<T>.
The collection selector overload returns a dictionary.

questions.text: How is a pure function defined in functional programming, and why is it valuable in C# lambdas?

options.text: A pure function must be declared static.
A pure function always returns the same output for the same input and has no side effects, making lambdas that are pure predictable, testable, and safe to compose or parallelize.
A pure function cannot capture any variables.
A pure function runs in a sandboxed context with no access to the runtime.

questions.text: What is delegate covariance in C# return type variance?

options.text: A delegate can accept a method returning a more derived type than declared in the delegate signature.
A delegate can be assigned a method whose return type is a derived class of the delegate's declared return type (covariant return type), since the returned value can always be used as the base type.
Delegates do not support covariance in any form.
Covariance applies only to generic delegates, not named delegates.

questions.text: In C#, what is an event accessor (add/remove), and when would you implement custom accessors?

options.text: Event accessors are properties of the EventArgs class.

Custom add/remove accessors let you control subscription logic (e.g., using a dictionary of handlers, thread-safe locking, or filtering subscriptions) instead of the auto-generated backing delegate field.
add/remove accessors are required for all events in C#.
They are used to serialize event handlers to XML.

questions.text: What does Task.WhenAll() do and how does it differ from awaiting tasks sequentially?

options.text: Task.WhenAll() runs tasks one at a time and awaits each.
Task.WhenAll() returns a task that completes when all supplied tasks complete, allowing all tasks to run concurrently rather than one-after-another, reducing total elapsed time.
Task.WhenAll() cancels all tasks if one fails.
Task.WhenAll() returns as soon as the first task completes.

questions.text: How do you handle exceptions from multiple tasks when using Task.WhenAll()?

options.text: Each task's exception must be caught with an individual try/catch.

Awaiting Task.WhenAll() throws an AggregateException (unwrapped by await to the first inner exception); to inspect all exceptions, examine the Task.Exception.InnerExceptions collection on the awaited task.
Task.WhenAll() swallows all exceptions unless you use .Result.
You must pass a CancellationToken to capture exceptions from WhenAll.

questions.text: What is the 'dynamic' keyword in C# and how does it differ from 'object'?

options.text: dynamic and object are identical; only the name differs.
dynamic bypasses static type checking and resolves member access at runtime via the DLR; object requires explicit casting and all member access is checked at compile time.
dynamic is only available in .NET Framework, not .NET Core.
dynamic forces boxing of all value types; object does not.

questions.text: What are expression-bodied members in C# and how do they relate to expression trees?

options.text: Expression-bodied members create expression trees automatically.

Expression-bodied members use '=>' syntax to provide a single-expression body for methods/properties (compiled to IL). Expression trees (Expression<Func<T>>) represent code as data. They use similar syntax but serve different purposes.
They are the same concept and interchangeable.
Expression-bodied members are only for read-only properties.

questions.text: What does the [StructLayout(LayoutKind.Sequential)] attribute control?

options.text: It makes a class behave like a struct.
It instructs the runtime to lay out struct or class fields in memory in declaration order, which is important for P/Invoke interop with native code.
It enables automatic struct packing to the smallest alignment.
It prevents the JIT from reordering struct fields.

questions.text: What extension method pattern enables type-safe 'Is' and 'As' operations without reflection?

options.text: Using dynamic dispatch to test types at runtime.
Defining extension methods with generic constraints: public static bool Is<T>(this object o) => o is T; and public static T As<T>(this object o) where T : class => o as T;.
Using Activator.CreateInstance for type testing.
Extension methods cannot implement type-testing patterns.

questions.text: How do you use an Expression<Func<T, bool>> as a predicate in Entity Framework LINQ queries?

options.text: You must call .Compile() first to convert it to a delegate.
Pass it directly to .Where(), since IQueryable<T>.Where() accepts Expression<Func<T,bool>>, allowing the expression tree to be translated to SQL by the EF provider.
Expression predicates cannot be used with Entity Framework.
Call .AsEnumerable() first, then pass the compiled delegate.

questions.text: What is ExpandoObject in C# and what interface does it implement?

options.text: ExpandoObject is a static dictionary class; it implements IDictionary.
ExpandoObject is a dynamic object that allows adding and removing members at runtime; it implements IDynamicMetaObjectProvider and IDictionary<string,object>.
ExpandoObject is a value type; it implements IStruct.
ExpandoObject only supports string-typed properties.

questions.text: What does [CallerMemberName] attribute do when applied to an optional string parameter?

options.text: It captures the assembly name at compile time.
It causes the compiler to automatically supply the name of the calling member (method, property, etc.) as the argument, enabling logging or INotifyPropertyChanged without hard-coded string names.
It retrieves the member name via reflection at runtime.
It restricts the method to being called only from class members.

questions.text: When using LINQ's Zip with three sequences (C# 9+), what happens if sequences have different lengths?

options.text: It throws an ArgumentException if lengths differ.
Zip stops producing elements when the shortest sequence is exhausted; remaining elements of longer sequences are ignored.
It pads shorter sequences with default values.
Zip requires all sequences to have the same length.

questions.text: What is the role of the Dynamic Language Runtime (DLR) in C#?

options.text: The DLR replaces the CLR for interpreted languages.
The DLR is a runtime layer that provides dynamic dispatch, late binding, and expression tree evaluation, enabling the 'dynamic' keyword and hosting dynamic languages like IronPython on .NET.
The DLR compiles C# source code at runtime.
The DLR handles only COM interop scenarios.

questions.text: What does reflection's Type.GetGenericArguments() return?

options.text: The generic constraints applied to the type parameters.
An array of Type objects representing the type arguments of a constructed generic type (e.g., for List<int>, it returns [typeof(int)]).
The names of the generic parameters as strings.
The base type of each generic argument.

questions.text: What is lazy evaluation in LINQ and how can it lead to unexpected re-execution?

options.text: LINQ queries are evaluated once and cached.
LINQ queries are deferred (lazy) and re-executed every time they are enumerated; if the source data changes between enumerations, different results are returned.
LINQ queries evaluate eagerly when any operator is appended.
Lazy evaluation only applies to GroupBy and OrderBy.

questions.text: What is the Delegate.Remove() method used for?

options.text: It removes all delegates from a multicast invocation list.
It removes the last occurrence of a specified delegate from a multicast delegate's invocation list, returning a new delegate; the -= operator on events uses this.
It unregisters a delegate from the DLR.
It disposes the delegate and frees its memory.

questions.text: What is function composition in C# functional programming?

options.text: Calling multiple functions in sequence inside a single method.
Creating a new function by combining two functions f and g such that compose(f,g)(x) = f(g(x)); each function's output becomes the next function's input.
Combining method groups into a single multicast delegate.
Using Aggregate to chain function calls over a collection.

questions.text: What does Task.WhenAny() return and in what scenario is it most useful?

options.text: Task.WhenAny() returns a Task<Task> that completes when all tasks finish.
Task.WhenAny() returns a Task<Task> that completes as soon as any one of the supplied tasks completes; useful for timeouts, racing multiple operations, or processing results as they arrive.
Task.WhenAny() cancels remaining tasks after the first completes.
Task.WhenAny() throws if the first completing task has faulted.

questions.text: What is an event memory leak in C# and which common pattern causes it?

options.text: Event memory leaks are caused by raising events with no subscribers.

A long-lived publisher holds a reference to subscribers via its event delegate; if a short-lived subscriber subscribes but never unsubscribes, the publisher prevents GC of the subscriber, causing a memory leak.
Events leak memory when declared as static fields.
Event memory leaks occur only in .NET Framework, not .NET 6+.

questions.text: Which overload of Expression.Constant() is used to create a typed constant node?

options.text: Expression.Constant(value) always infers the type.
Expression.Constant(value, type) lets you specify the type explicitly, which is important when the value is null or needs to be treated as an interface/base type rather than the concrete type.
Expression.Literal(value) is the correct method for constants.
Constants are always inlined; Expression.Constant is never needed.

questions.text: How do you add a new property to an ExpandoObject and read it back dynamically?

options.text: expando.AddProperty("Name", "Alice"); var name = expando.GetProperty("Name");
dynamic expando = new ExpandoObject(); expando.Name = "Alice"; string name = expando.Name;
ExpandoObject.Declare("Name"); expando["Name"] = "Alice";
ExpandoObject requires a schema to be defined before setting properties.

questions.text: What is the purpose of [InternalsVisibleTo] attribute?

options.text: It makes all internal members public in the declaring assembly.
It grants a specified friend assembly access to the declaring assembly's internal types and members, commonly used to expose internals to unit test projects.
It controls which namespaces are visible within the assembly.
It merges two assemblies at link time.

questions.text: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle's implication for delegate parameter variance (contravariance)?

options.text: A delegate expecting a Base parameter cannot accept a method taking a Derived parameter.

A delegate declared as Action<Base> can reference a method that takes Action<Derived> is invalid; it's the reverse — Action<object> can be used where Action<string> is expected (contravariance), because a handler for any object can handle a string.
Contravariance means a derived type can be used where a base type is expected.
Delegate variance is not related to the Liskov Substitution Principle.

questions.text: How can reflection be used to find all types in an assembly that implement a specific interface?

options.text: Assembly.GetTypes().Where(t => t.Name.Contains("IMyInterface"))
Assembly.GetTypes().Where(t => typeof(IMyInterface).IsAssignableFrom(t) && !t.IsInterface)
Assembly.Scan(typeof(IMyInterface))
You cannot scan an assembly for interface implementations via reflection.

questions.text: What is a thread-safe event invocation pattern in C# to avoid the 'null check race condition'?

options.text: Always invoke events inside a lock statement.

Copy the event delegate to a local variable before null-checking and invoking: var handler = MyEvent; handler?.Invoke(this, args); This avoids a race where the event becomes null between the check and invocation.
Use Interlocked.Exchange to null the event before invoking.
Events are inherently thread-safe; no special pattern is needed.

questions.text: What is the difference between Func<T, TResult> and Expression<Func<T, TResult>>?

options.text: They are interchangeable; you can use either in any context.

Func<T,TResult> is a compiled, directly-invokable delegate; Expression<Func<T,TResult>> is a data structure representing the code as a tree that can be inspected, modified, or translated (e.g., to SQL) before optionally being compiled.
Expression<Func<T,TResult>> executes faster than Func<T,TResult>.
Func<T,TResult> can hold expression trees internally.
